skill.name: get-available-resources
skill.description: Detects local CPU, GPU, memory, disk, and operating system resources before scientific computing work. It writes a resource report and recommends parallel processing, memory, GPU, and large data strategies.
skill.summary: Detect local compute resources and choose practical scientific computing strategies.
skill.author: K-Dense-AI

security_audit.summary: Static findings for markdown backticks, weak cryptography, and dynamic import are false positives after review. The remaining concerns are legitimate but sensitive resource detection behavior: the script executes hardcoded local utilities and writes system inventory details to a JSON file.

medium_findings.title: Local System Resource Inventory
medium_findings.description: The skill intentionally collects CPU, memory, disk, OS, and GPU details to recommend scientific computing strategies. This is documented behavior and no network exfiltration was found, but the report may expose host characteristics if shared.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The resource inventory is implemented directly and matches the skill purpose. Risk is limited because the data is written locally and I found no evidence of network transmission.

medium_findings.title: External System Utility Execution
medium_findings.description: The script runs local platform utilities for GPU and hardware detection. Commands and arguments are hardcoded list values without shell execution, reducing injection risk, but the skill still depends on executing binaries from the local environment.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess calls are clearly present and semantically needed for hardware detection. They are not command injection patterns because there is no shell and no user-controlled command string.

low_findings.title: User-Controlled Local Output Path
low_findings.description: The CLI accepts an output path and writes the resource report there. This is expected functionality, but users should avoid pointing it at sensitive files or shared locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The output argument and file write are visible in the script. The risk is low because writing the chosen report path is the documented behavior.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer False Positives
low_findings.description: Markdown backticks in SKILL.md were flagged as shell execution, and weak cryptography alerts did not match cryptographic code. The dynamic import at line 255 imports datetime with a hardcoded module name.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited markdown locations are formatting or examples, not executable Ruby or shell. The dynamic import uses a fixed standard library module name, not untrusted input.

dangerous_patterns.title: subprocess.run for Resource Detection
dangerous_patterns.description: The script uses subprocess.run to call nvidia-smi, rocm-smi, sysctl, and system_profiler. The commands are hardcoded and use timeouts, but external command execution remains a sensitive capability.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: These calls are directly present in the script and are semantically tied to GPU and platform detection. No evidence of user-controlled command injection was found.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local JSON Report Write
dangerous_patterns.description: The script writes detected resource details to a local JSON file. This supports the skill, but the file can contain host details such as OS version, hardware type, disk path, and memory capacity.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The write operation is explicit and stores the generated resource dictionary. The sensitivity depends on where users store or share the report.

content.user_title: Detect Compute Resources Before Scientific Work
content.value_statement: Scientific tasks can fail when hardware limits are unknown. This skill checks local compute resources and recommends practical processing strategies.
actual_capabilities: Detects CPU core counts, architecture, processor details, and available frequency data.
Reports total memory, available memory, swap space, and memory usage percentage.
Checks disk capacity and free space for the current working directory.
Detects NVIDIA, AMD, and Apple Silicon GPU backends when local utilities are available.
Writes a local resource report with strategy recommendations for scientific computing.
Suggests worker counts, GPU libraries, and large data handling approaches.
limitations: GPU detection depends on installed local utilities such as nvidia-smi, rocm-smi, or system_profiler.
Resource values are a snapshot and can change as other processes start or stop.
The recommendations use general thresholds and do not benchmark actual workload performance.
The generated report may contain local host details that should not be shared casually.

use_cases.title: Plan Large Dataset Processing
use_cases.description: Check memory and disk capacity before choosing pandas, Dask, Zarr, HDF5, or streaming workflows.

use_cases.title: Select Training Hardware
use_cases.description: Detect CUDA, ROCm, Metal, or CPU-only environments before configuring model training libraries.

use_cases.title: Tune Parallel Jobs
use_cases.description: Use detected CPU cores to choose a practical worker count for simulations or batch processing.

prompt_templates.title: Check Resources
prompt_templates.prompt: Use get-available-resources before this analysis and summarize the available CPU, memory, disk, and GPU resources.

prompt_templates.title: Choose Data Strategy
prompt_templates.prompt: Run get-available-resources, then recommend whether this dataset should use in-memory loading, chunking, or out-of-core processing.

prompt_templates.title: Configure Model Training
prompt_templates.prompt: Use get-available-resources to detect accelerator support, then recommend the best training device and framework options.

prompt_templates.title: Design Compute Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Run get-available-resources and use the report to design a parallel, memory-aware workflow for this scientific pipeline.

output_examples.input: Check this machine before I process a large genomics dataset.
output: The machine has multiple CPU cores and enough disk space for intermediate files.
Available memory is moderate, so chunked loading is safer than loading the full dataset.
Use Dask or Zarr for the main processing path and reserve memory for downstream analysis.

output_examples.input: Find out whether I can train this model with GPU acceleration.
output: A GPU backend is available, so training can use an accelerated device.
Select the framework build that matches the detected backend.
Keep a CPU fallback path for environments without the same accelerator.

best_practices: Run the skill before computationally intensive work, not after resource errors occur.
Review the generated report before sharing it because it contains local host details.
Re-run detection when switching machines, containers, notebooks, or long-running sessions.
anti_patterns: Assuming one resource report stays accurate after system load changes.
Using maximum CPU workers without leaving capacity for the operating system.
Sharing the generated resource file in public logs or repositories without review.

faq.question: What does this skill detect?
faq.answer: It detects CPU, memory, disk, operating system, Python version, and supported GPU backend details.

faq.question: Does it send resource data to a server?
faq.answer: No evidence found of network transmission. The script writes a local JSON report.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: It executes local system utilities and records host details, which is sensitive even when used for a valid purpose.

faq.question: Can it detect every GPU?
faq.answer: No. It depends on local utilities and drivers for NVIDIA, AMD, or Apple Silicon detection.

faq.question: Should I commit the output file?
faq.answer: Review it first. It can include machine details such as OS version, disk path, and hardware information.

faq.question: Does it replace benchmarking?
faq.answer: No. It provides planning guidance, but workload benchmarks are still needed for final performance decisions.
